text.title: GitHub Actions — Matrix Builds Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules including a `skip-this-methodology` rule. Each pairs a binary check with sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Matrix axes MUST be explicit named lists (`python-version: [3.10, 3.11, 3.12]`). Wildcards or implicit fallbacks rejected.
rule.rationale: Implicit axes drift silently when GHA updates; explicit lists are auditable and reproducible.

rule.statement: Use `include` to add specific combinations (e.g. one extra job for python 3.12 on windows-only) and `exclude` to skip combinations that don't make sense (e.g. mac on python 3.10 is unsupported). Both are version-controlled.
rule.rationale: Without include/exclude the matrix is N×M; the right matrix is often a sparse subset.

rule.statement: `fail-fast` MUST be set explicitly. Default `true` is fine for cheap matrices; set `false` when each combination's failure carries unique signal (cross-OS compat).
rule.rationale: Default fail-fast hides cross-OS issues by killing the first failure; deliberate choice is required.

rule.statement: `max-parallel` MUST be set when matrix size >10 to avoid burning runner budget; align with GitHub plan concurrency limit.
rule.rationale: Unbounded parallelism in big matrices exhausts the runner queue and starves other workflows.

rule.statement: Per-combination artifacts MUST be named with the combination key (e.g. `coverage-${{matrix.os}}-${{matrix.python}}`) to avoid overwrite conflicts.
rule.rationale: Unscoped names cause silent overwrite; the last job wins and others' data is lost.

rule.statement: Skip when matrix is 1×1 OR tests cannot run concurrently OR runner-budget cost outweighs coverage value.
rule.rationale: Without true combinatorial coverage the matrix degrades to single-job-with-extra-yaml.
